actions.thought: I am in the Small Blind (Button) for the first hand of the match. While Heads-Up strategy dictates playing a very wide range of hands (often 80%+), 6d 2h is in the absolute bottom of starting hands (bottom 5%). It lacks connectivity, high card value, and suit coordination. Although I would have position post-flop, the hand has very poor playability and is virtually impossible to play profitably against a call or a 3-bet. Since I have no reads on the opponent yet, taking the low-variance route and folding the bottom of my range is the standard and correct play.

Holding 2d 7s on the button, which is the mathematically weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. In a heads-up match against an opponent labeled 'Pro', there is little incentive to play this hand, even for a half-blind discount, as it performs poorly post-flop and lacks playability. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve the stack for more favorable situations.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 5c 2c. In heads-up play, 52s is one of the weakest suited hands. While I have position post-flop, the hand has very poor playability and low equity against a random hand. Standard charts suggest folding this hand pre-flop most of the time. Given it is early in the match and I have no reads to justify a loose open or limp, folding is the most prudent action to preserve chips.

T5o is a weak hand to open from the small blind. While I am on the button, this hand doesn't play well post-flop and doesn't have enough equity to justify a raise or a limp against a competent big blind. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold QJo on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match. This is a strong starting hand, well within the top percentage of hands suitable for opening. In heads-up play, it is important to play aggressively from the button to capitalize on positional advantage. The previous hands have been passive, consisting mostly of folds. I want to seize the initiative and build the pot. A standard raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is appropriate here to extract value and deny equity to marginal hands in the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Seven offsuit. The opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 250, which is a standard 2.5x open. Given the pot odds of 3.33 to 1 (calling 150 to win a total pot of 500), K7o is a strong enough hand to defend against a wide button/SB opening range. I will call to see the flop and play post-flop with my high-card value.

I have King-high on a paired board of 8-4-8. As the defender in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. King-high has some showdown value, but the board doesn't hit my range particularly hard, nor does it necessarily hit the SB's range. I will check to see their action and keep the pot manageable.

I hold QdJs on a flop of 8h 4c 8c. The pot is 500. My opponent (BB) checked. This paired board texture interacts decently with the Big Blind's calling range (which contains many 8x and 4x combos). While I have range advantage in terms of overpairs (AA-99), my specific hand is just two overcards. Betting might fold out pure air, but checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting a Queen or Jack on the turn. It also prevents me from getting check-raised off a hand that has decent showdown value against missed draws. Pot control is preferred here.

The turn is a Queen of Spades, bringing the board to 8h-4c-8c-Qs. After both players checked the flop, my King-high still retains some showdown value, though the Queen is a card that could easily hit the opponent's pre-flop raising range. I will check to maintain pot control and see how the opponent acts. There is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff yet, nor is it strong enough to bet for value.

On the Turn, the board is 8h 4c 8c Qs. I have improved to Top Pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker. The pot is 500. My opponent (Big Blind) checked to me again. The flop action (check-check) suggests my opponent likely does not have an 8, as they often would have bet or check-raised to protect against draws or build the pot. Now that I have a strong value hand, I need to bet to extract value from worse hands (like a 4, pocket pairs 55-77, or a weaker Queen) and to charge club draws. A check-back here would be a mistake as it allows draws to realize equity for free and misses value. I will bet 300 (60% of the pot), which is a standard size to get called by worse hands while protecting against draws.

The opponent checked the flop but is now betting 300 into a 500 pot on a Queen turn. The Queen is a card that hits the pre-flop raiser's range significantly harder than mine (containing hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, QT). King-high has some theoretical showdown value, but it is unlikely to be best here given the betting action. Calling 300 to win 800 requires approximately 27% equity, and with only King-high and no significant draw, I do not have sufficient equity to continue. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 64o. While heads-up play allows for a wider opening range, 64o is one of the weakest hands in the deck. Given that it's early in the tournament and I want to maintain my stack for better opportunities, I will choose to fold this hand rather than entering a pot out of position or with a significant range disadvantage against a 'Pro' opponent.

Current hand is Ten-Five offsuit (T5o) in the Small Blind (Button). In Heads-Up play, while the Button should play a very wide range (often 80%+), T5o falls into the bottom 10-20% of hands usually recommended for folding. Although I have the positional advantage post-flop, the hand has poor playability (unsuited, gapped, low kicker). Looking at the match history, play has been relatively tight with several walks. Folding here is consistent with my previous actions of folding trash hands (26o, 52o) to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots early in the tournament.

QTo is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the average range. Being on the button, I want to play aggressively to gain the initiative and build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential. A standard 3x raise to 300 is an appropriate sizing here.

I hold Pocket Fours (4c 4h) in the Big Blind against a Button raise to 300 (3bb). In heads-up play, pocket pairs have decent value against a wide opening range. The pot odds require me to call 200 to win a total pot of 600 (33% equity needed), and 44 has approximately 50-55% equity against a standard heads-up opening range. While playing a low pair out of position is difficult post-flop, folding is too tight and 3-betting bloats the pot with a hand that plays poorly against 4-bets or calls. Calling allows me to set-mine or evaluate the flop texture.

I have flopped bottom set on a dry, rainbow board (Ad 4s 8h). This is an incredibly strong hand in heads-up play. The Ace on the board interacts very well with the Button's opening range, making it highly probable that my opponent will continuation bet if they hold an Ace, or bluff if they missed. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their air and is generally suboptimal on this texture. My goal is to extract maximum value. The best way to do this is to check to the aggressor. If they bet, I can decide between check-calling (to keep bluffs and weaker made hands in) or check-raising. Given the dryness of the board, I have no immediate need to protect my hand, so checking is the clear first step.

The board [Ad, 4s, 8h] is very dry and favors my pre-flop raising range as the button. While I only have Queen-high, continuation betting (C-betting) on an Ace-high board is a standard high-frequency play for the aggressor. Pro's check is expected, and a small bet of 200 (1/3 pot) allows me to take down the pot immediately if he missed, while representing a strong range.

I hold bottom set (44) on a dry, rainbow board of Ad-4s-8h. My opponent, who raised pre-flop and is now c-betting 1/3 pot (200 into 600), likely has a wide range that includes top pair (Ax), middle pairs, and plenty of air/bluffs. Raising here is suboptimal because the board is so dry; a check-raise looks incredibly strong and would likely fold out all his bluffs and weak made hands, leaving me only against hands that might dominate me (though unlikely) or simply ending the action when I have a monster. Calling is the superior play to keep his bluffs in the pot and allow him to barrel the turn or improve to a second-best hand. There are very few scare cards on the turn, so I can afford to slow-play to extract maximum value.

I hold a set of Fours on a Turn board of Ad-4s-8h-6c. The board remains relatively dry, although a 5-7 straight is now possible. My hand is extremely strong, and my goal is to maximize value from my opponent's range, which likely includes top pair (Ax), draws picked up on the turn, or pure bluffs. Leading out (donk betting) here would look suspicious and likely fold out all his bluffs and weak hands. By checking, I give my opponent the opportunity to fire a second barrel with his strong hands (value betting Ax or better) or continue bluffing. If he bets, I can consider a check-raise to start building the pot towards an all-in, as the pot is currently small relative to the stacks. Therefore, checking is the most profitable play to induce action.

The 6c on the turn is largely a blank card that doesn't significantly alter the board texture. Having been called on the flop, Pro's range likely includes marginal Aces, 8x, or pocket pairs. Since I currently only have Queen-high and limited outs, firing a second barrel into a range that likely contains an Ace is low-frequency and risky. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and see the river for free, potentially realizing some equity or finding a better spot to bluff or fold depending on Pro's river action.

I hold a set of Fours on a board of Ad-4s-8h-6c-Qc. My opponent checked back the turn, which usually indicates a capped range consisting of marginal made hands (like weak Aces, pocket pairs, or an 8) or a give-up. The River Queen may have improved a floating broadway hand to second pair. If I check, my opponent is very likely to check back hands like Ax or Qx to realize their showdown value cheaply, causing me to miss out on value. To maximize my profit, I need to lead out. A bet of 600 (60% pot) is large enough to build the pot but small enough to get called by top pair (Ax) or a rivered Queen. I am beating almost his entire range, losing only to the unlikely 57 straight or higher sets which would have likely played differently.

On the river, the Qc significantly improves my hand to a pair of Queens. The action sequence is telling: Pro check-called a small bet on an Ace-high flop, then both players checked a blank turn. Pro's lead for 600 on the river after the Queen hits is polarized. He could have a slow-played Ace, but he could also be bluffing or betting a worse pair (like an 8 or a lower pocket pair) thinking I am weak after checking back the turn. Given that I've improved to second pair with a decent kicker (Ten), and I only need to be right about 27% of the time to justify the call (600 to win 2200), calling is the most profitable decision here. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (like an Ace).

I hold J2o on the button (SB), which is near the absolute bottom of the starting hand range. Even in heads-up play where ranges are wide (typically opening 70-80%), J2o is usually a fold due to its lack of playability and reverse implied odds. My opponent has shown some calling tendencies (Hand #8), making bluffing with trash less attractive. Folding preserves my chip lead and avoids difficult post-flop spots with a weak hand.

I am on the button with T6o. While this is a relatively weak hand, in heads-up play, the button should be played aggressively, especially since Pro has shown a tendency to fold frequently in previous hands. Raising here allows me to pick up the blinds immediately or take control of the pot early. Given Pro's tight play so far, a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 4d, facing a 3x raise (300 total) from the Button. This is a very weak hand (trash), even in heads-up play. Although the pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 400), my hand has poor playability and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. While I should defend my big blind frequently against button opens, 94o is near the bottom of my range and is a standard fold.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with J5o. This is a weak, disconnected, unsuited hand. Although heads-up play requires a wide range (often VPIP > 80%), J5o sits near the bottom of that range. Given the deep stacks (87 BB effective), reverse implied odds and playability issues make this a standard fold, consistent with my previous folds of J2o and T5o.

I am on the button with Jack-Six offsuit. While a marginal hand, in heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is important. Pro has shown a tendency to fold their small blind frequently, and I have successfully stolen the blinds with a 3x raise in recent hands. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and attempt to win the pot pre-flop or control the hand with position.

The opponent has raised to 3x the big blind (300 chips) from the Button. In a Heads-Up match, Q8o is well within the standard defense range for the Big Blind. I need to call 200 more chips to win a total pot of 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds (requiring ~33% equity). Against a typical wide Button opening range (even with the larger sizing), Q8o has roughly 45-50% equity. While playing out of position is difficult, folding here would be too tight. I will call and evaluate the flop.

On this A-3-4 two-tone board, the texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor who has a significant range advantage with all the strong Ax combinations. As the defender in the Big Blind with Queen-high and no direct draws (flush or straight), I have very little equity. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands or isolate me against strength. The standard play is to check to the aggressor and evaluate their action. If they bet, I will likely have to fold given my lack of pair or draw.

As the pre-flop aggressor on an Ace-high board, I have a significant range advantage. This flop (As 3h 4s) is very favorable for my perceived range. Pro has checked to me, and a continuation bet is the standard play here to maintain initiative and represent top pair or better. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is an efficient sizing to fold out Pro's air or weak hands while keeping the pot manageable if they decide to call.

I am holding Q8o on an As-3h-4s board. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), who has a range advantage containing all strong Aces and flush draws. My opponent has continued with a half-pot bet of 300. I have Queen-high with no pair and no meaningful draw (no flush draw, no straight draw). My equity against a standard c-betting range is well below the roughly 25% required to call based on pot odds. Continuing out of position with essentially air is losing strategy. The correct play is to fold.

QJo is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, typically well ahead of the Big Blind's random range. Being in position (Button/SB), my goal is to build the pot and seize the initiative. I previously raised QJo to 250 in Hand #5 successfully. A raise to 250 (2.5x BB) puts pressure on the opponent and extracts value from weaker holdings.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6c. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 250 from the Button. Looking at previous hands, Pro has shown extremely tight pre-flop tendencies, even folding Ace-King offsuit from the Button in Hand #11. This suggests that when Pro does raise, their range is likely very strong. 8-6 offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, and against a potentially tight range, it is not worth defending. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Jh3c is a weak hand with little connectivity or post-flop potential. While I have the advantage of being on the button and acting last on subsequent streets, this hand is at the bottom of the range. Looking at previous hands, the opponent has demonstrated a willingness to call pre-flop raises in the big blind (Hands 8 and 12). Since J3o is unlikely to win at showdown or improve significantly, and I have folded similar hands like T5o and 46d in this position earlier, I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button (SB) with King-Queen offsuit (KQo), which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My previous play shows I have been raising to 250 with broadway hands like QJo (Hand #5, Hand #13). The opponent has folded to raises often. Raising to 250 extracts value, denies free equity to the Big Blind, and stays consistent with my established sizing for strong hands.

I have a pocket pair (4h 4c) in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 2.5x. While 44 is a vulnerable hand, in a heads-up match, any pocket pair is strong enough to continue. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially set mine or play if the board is favorable, without bloating the pot out of position.

The flop is 9h 6h 9s. I have pocket fours, which gives me two pair (nines and fours). Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to see how Pro reacts. This allows me to control the pot size and gauge the strength of Pro's hand on a board that could have easily missed their broadway range but hits their 9x or mid-pair holdings.

I hold King-Queen offsuit on a paired board of 9h-6h-9s. After raising pre-flop and getting called, the pot is 500. This board texture interacts well with the Big Blind's calling range (containing 9x, 6x, and straight draws). While I have two overcards and the Qh (providing a flush blocker and backdoor potential), my hand has showdown value that benefits from realizing equity rather than betting. Betting might fold out worse hands that I beat anyway, while isolating me against 9x or pairs. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, see a free turn card, and is consistent with my previous play on paired boards (Hand #5).

On the turn, the 4s gives me a full house (Fours full of Nines). The flop was checked through, which suggests Pro likely does not have a Nine or a strong pocket pair. By leading out now, I can get value from a Six, a heart flush draw, or a straight draw (like 7-8 or 5-7) that might have been checked back on the flop. A bet of 300 into a 500 pot is a standard value bet size that allows me to build the pot while still being small enough for weaker hands to consider calling.

I hold King-Queen offsuit on a paired, double-flush-draw board of 9h-6h-9s-4s. After I checked back the flop to control the pot, the opponent leads the turn for 300 into 500. This 'probe' bet is common with a wide range, including trips (9x), pairs (6x, 4x), and numerous draws (hearts, spades, straight draws). My King-high is currently ahead of all their semi-bluffs. Additionally, I have 6 outs (Kings and Queens) against lower pairs. Folding here would make me too exploitable after checking the flop. I need to defend my checking range, and the price (calling 300 to win a total pot of 1100, requiring ~27% equity) justifies a call to see the river and potentially win at showdown against missed draws.

I have improved to a full house (Fours full of Nines) on the turn. The river 3d is a complete blank that doesn't change the board texture or complete any draws. Since I led the turn and was called, it's likely Pro has a marginal hand like a Six, a mid-pocket pair, or perhaps a Nine they are playing slowly. I need to bet for value here. A bet of around 70-75% of the pot is large enough to get value from a Nine or a Six that doesn't want to fold, while still looking like a polarized range that includes some missed heart or spade draws.

On the River, the board reads 9h-6h-9s-4s-3d. The pot is 1100, and the opponent bets 800. I hold King-Queen offsuit (King High). The river 3d completes a straight for 5-7, though the flush draws (hearts and spades) have missed. 

The opponent's line (bet Turn, bet River) is polarizing. They represent a 9 (trips), a straight (57), or a made pair (6x, 4x, 3x) betting for value/protection, or a missed draw bluffing. 

Crucially, I hold the Qh. This is a bad card to have when bluff-catching because it blocks a significant portion of the opponent's bluffing range (missed heart flush draws). By having the Qh, I make it mathematically more likely the opponent holds a value hand or a different bluff (like missed straight draw or spades). 

Additionally, my hand is very weak; I lose to any pair and even Ace-high bluffs. Given the large bet size (requiring ~30% equity) and the fact that I block the bluffs I want the opponent to have, calling is negative EV. I have better hands in my range to defend with (like Ace-high or small pairs) that I would have checked back on the flop.

I am on the button with 84o, which is a very weak hand. While I want to maintain a high opening frequency in heads-up play, 84o is near the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop. Given that I have recently folded similar low-equity hands from the small blind to maintain a balanced but disciplined approach, I will fold this hand and wait for a better spot.

I hold a premium hand (AKo) on the button in a heads-up match. This is a top-tier starting hand that dominates most of the opponent's range. Looking at my history (Hands #5, #13, #15), I have consistently raised to 250 when opening from the button with broadway cards. To extract value while maintaining board coverage and disguising the strength of my hand, I will stick to my standard opening size of 2.5bb.

Pro has demonstrated an extremely tight pre-flop strategy from the button, even folding premium hands like AKo and A6s in previous hands. Given this tendency, Pro's 2.5x raise likely represents a very strong range. K3o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position and is likely dominated or significantly behind Pro's narrow opening range. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 6d 2h, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. While wide opening ranges are common in heads-up play, 6-2 offsuit lacks any real equity or post-flop playability. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack for more favorable spots, I will fold this hand instead of attempting a steal or completing the small blind.

I hold 8c 3h in the Small Blind (Button), which is an extremely weak hand (offsuit trash). While aggressive button play is standard in heads-up, this specific hand has very poor playability and equity if called. Recent history shows I have successfully raised or received walks in the last few hands, so my image is active. There is no need to force a play with the bottom of my range. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

The opponent has demonstrated an extremely tight pre-flop strategy, folding high-value hands like Ace-King and pocket nines. In a heads-up context, this is highly exploitable. While 8-4 offsuit is statistically weak, the opponent's high fold-to-blind-steal frequency makes raising from the button a high-frequency winning play regardless of my hole cards.

I am in the Big Blind with T8o. My opponent on the Button (Flash) raises to 300 (3bb). I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring about 25% realized equity to call. While T8o is a defendable hand against a min-raise in Heads-Up play, a 3x raise implies a slightly stronger range or simply offers worse odds. Looking at Flash's history, he has folded the button 6 times and raised 4 times in the documented hands, suggesting an opening frequency of around 40%. This is tighter than the standard 70%+ HU opening frequency. Against a tighter range (top 40%) and out of position for a larger price, T8o is likely -EV to call. I will fold.

I hold 6h 9c on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match. This is a 9-6 offsuit hand, which is statistically weak (bottom 25-30% of hands). While being on the button grants me position post-flop, 96o plays poorly and does not connect well with most boards. I have been playing a relatively solid strategy, folding trash hands from the SB. Without a specific read that the opponent is over-folding their Big Blind significantly, simply folding this weak holding is the standard and variance-minimizing play.

I am on the button with Jack-Four of hearts, a suited hand that plays well in position. Looking at the history of the match, the opponent (Pro) has been folding a high percentage of hands pre-flop. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind, which has been a successful strategy for taking down the pot early or setting up a continuation bet on the flop.

Opponent raises to 3bb (300). I need to call 200 into a pot of 400, requiring 33% equity. My hand 6h 5s is a low offsuit connector. While it has raw equity close to 35% against a generic opening range, the opponent has shown discipline by folding the button frequently (approx 50% of the time recently), suggesting a stronger-than-random range. Additionally, playing 65o out of position against a 3x raise suffers from poor equity realization. It is marginally -EV to call here compared to folding.

I hold 9c 5d in the Small Blind. This is an off-suit, unconnected hand with low card value, ranking near the bottom of starting hands in heads-up play (approximately bottom 15%). While I have a slight stack advantage, opening with such a weak hand is generally negative EV unless the opponent is over-folding significantly. My opponent, Flash, has defended their blind against raises occasionally (e.g., Hand 15) but has folded to aggression a few times. However, with 95o, the post-flop playability is very poor if called. Maintaining a polarized or merged opening range is better than opening pure trash. The standard and most prudent play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 93o. In heads-up play, the button should generally be raising a wide range of hands to put pressure on the big blind. Looking at the history of the match, Pro has shown a tendency to fold their big blind to my 3x raises (Hands 10, 20, 22) or fold early on the flop (Hand 12). While 93o is a weak hand, the high frequency of folds from Pro makes a steal attempt profitable. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing.

I hold A5o in the Big Blind against a Button raise to 300 (3x BB). My opponent, Flash, has consistently used this 3x sizing for opens, suggesting a standard wide opening range rather than a specific strength tell. I am getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring roughly 33% equity. A5o has approximately 53-59% equity against a typical wide heads-up opening range and around 43% even against a tighter range. Therefore, folding is too tight. While a 3-bet is a viable option due to the Ace blocker, calling is the standard low-variance play to realize my equity out of position given the stack depth (100 BBs). I will call to see a flop.

I am out of position on a paired flop of [Tc 7s Th] with Ace-high. This board texture is relatively dry but favors the pre-flop raiser slightly in terms of overpairs, though I have plenty of Tx in my defense range. Leading out (donk betting) with Ace-high has little utility; it folds out worse hands (like K-high) and gets action from better hands. My goal is to realize my showdown value or catch a pair on later streets cheaply. Checking is the standard optimal play to the pre-flop aggressor here.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board came paired with Tens. This is a good texture for a continuation bet because it is difficult for the Big Blind to have connected strongly unless they have a Ten or a pocket pair. Since Pro checked, I will bet 300 (half the pot) to represent a strong range and attempt to take the pot down immediately, as my 9-high has very little showdown value.

I hold Ace-high (A5) on a paired board of [Tc 7s Th]. My opponent, who opened from the button, has continuation bet 300 (half pot). In heads-up play, ranges are wide, and this board texture misses a large portion of the button's opening range. Ace-high is currently ahead of all non-paired hands like KQ, KJ, QJ, and suited connectors. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, requiring only 25% equity. Given I have two outs to a pair of Aces or Fives (likely winning) and currently beat all pure bluffs, folding is too exploitable. Raising turns a hand with showdown value into a bluff, which isn't necessary. Calling is the standard play to defend the Big Blind and realize equity.

I hold Ace-high (A5) on a board of [Tc 7s Th 8d]. After check-calling the flop, the turn card [8d] adds some connectivity (straight possibilities with 9, J) and connects with the opponent's wide button range (pairs of 8s, straight draws). Leading out here has negative expected value as it folds out the bluffs I am currently beating (like KQ, KJ, lower air) and only gets action from better hands (Tx, pairs of 7s/8s, overpairs). Checking is the standard play to keep the opponent's range wide, induce bluffs, or hopefully see a free river card to realize my showdown value.

I have picked up an open-ended straight draw on the turn (needing a 6 or a Jack) with my 9s and the board of 10-7-10-8. Pro called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they have some part of this board, such as a 7, a pair, or a float with Ace-high. Since Pro has checked the turn, I have the option to take a free card to try and hit my straight. Betting here might not force a fold from a 7 or a pair of 8s, and checking back allows me to realize my equity without risk of being check-raised. I will check.

I hold Ace-high (Ah 5s) on a final board of [Tc 7s Th 8d Qh]. After calling the flop c-bet and seeing the turn go check-check, the opponent's range is likely capped at one-pair hands or missed draws, though the river Queen is a significant scare card. The Queen connects with broadway floats (like KQ, QJ) and completes the J9 straight, making it a favorable card for the opponent's range rather than mine. Leading out as a bluff is low-percentage because the Q improves many hands that checked the turn (pairs of Qs, straights). My hand has minimal showdown value but beats pure air. The standard play is to check, intending to fold to a bet, or hope the opponent checks back a worse hand like K-high.

The Queen of Hearts on the river does not improve my hand. My open-ended straight draw from the turn (needing a Jack or a Six) has missed. Pro checked the river after calling my flop continuation bet and checking the turn, which typically indicates a hand with some showdown value like a 7, an 8, or a weak pocket pair that is hoping to reach showdown cheaply. Because I checked the turn, my range appears capped, and a bluff on the river is unlikely to fold out these marginal hands. I will check and concede the pot, as 9-high has no chance of winning at showdown unless Pro has a lower 9-high or lower, which is improbable given the flop call.

I hold 9hTd on the Button (SB), which is a respectable hand in heads-up play, offering straight potential and high-card strength relative to a random hand. In previous hands, I have established a standard opening raise size of 2.5BB (250), which has successfully bought the pot or allowed me to play in position. Opponent 'Flash' has shown a tendency to call raises from the BB (Hands 5, 15) rather than aggressively 3-betting, making a raise a profitable play to build the pot while retaining the initiative and positional advantage. Folding is too passive for this holding, and raising is superior to limping to deny free equity to the Big Blind.

I am holding 7-3 offsuit in the Big Blind. This is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em and has very poor playability. My opponent (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds (150 to call into a 400 pot), I will be out of position for the entire hand with a trash hand. Leading out here (betting) is unlikely to get value from worse hands and might only fold out hands I am already beating (like Ace-high), while keeping me in against better hands. Checking allows me to realize my equity cheaply, keeps the opponent's bluffs in their range, and gives me an easy call if they bet given my pair plus flush draw outs. The river card is the Ace of Spades, making the final board Kc Qc 5s 6d As. This is a bad card for my hand (pair of Fives) and a bad card to bluff. Checking is the only viable option; if the opponent checks back, I might occasionally win against total air, but if they bet, I can easily fold.

On a board with three overcards (Ace, King, Queen), my pocket eights have some showdown value but are not strong enough to bet for value.
